text: The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position with a 30-degree Trendelenburg tilt to the right side, preparing for the robotic prostatectomy procedure.
The patient is positioned in a modified lithotomy position with a 30-degree Trendelenburg tilt to the right side, preparing for the robotic colorectal surgery.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery near the aorta, using a medial to lateral approach with bipolar and monopolar energy devices.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery near the aorta, using a medial to lateral approach with bipolar and monopolar energy devices.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein using surgical clips, securing the vessel before transection.
The surgeon divides the inferior mesenteric vein using surgical clips, securing the vessel before proceeding with further dissection.

The surgeon mobilizes the entire left colon and splenic flexure, performing a gentle incision of the Girota fascia using monopolar scissors. The colorepiploid dissection is completed, facilitating the splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon mobilizes the entire left colon and left splenic flexure, performing an incision of the Girota fascia using monopolar scissors. The colopiploid dissection is completed, achieving splenic flexure mobilization.

The surgeon performs total mesorectal excision using the da Vinci robotic system, beginning with dissection in the posterior plane of the mesorectum, followed by the right and left lateral planes, and concluding with the anterior plane.

The surgeon performs total mesorectal excision, beginning with dissection in the posterior plane of the mesorectum, followed by the right and left lateral planes, and concluding with the anterior plane.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic endogea device to divide the rectum, recharging the device twice to facilitate a mechanical coloanal anastomosis. Prior to this, the urology team performs a radical prostatectomy and a large iliac lymphadenectomy, beginning the dissection at the right iliac artery.

The surgeon divides the rectum using a laparoscopic endogea device, recharging it twice to facilitate a mechanical coloanal anastomosis. Prior to this, the urology team performs a radical prostatectomy and a large iliac lymphadenectomy, beginning the dissection at the right iliac artery.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis between the bladder neck and the membranous urethra following a radical prostatectomy, using two running sutures with a V-lock suture device.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis between the bladder neck and the membranous urethra following a radical prostatectomy, using two running sutures secured with a B-lock device.

The surgeon extracts the surgical specimen through a six-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision. Following the division between the descending colon and sigmoid colon, a purse-string suture is placed to facilitate the positioning of a 29-millimeter anvil for an end-to-end anastomosis.

The surgeon extracts the surgical specimen through a six-centimeter Pfannenstiel incision. Following the division between the descending colon and the sigmoid colon, a purse-string suture is applied to facilitate the positioning of a 29-millimeter anvil for an end-to-end anastomosis.
The surgeon completes the procedure by performing an end-to-end staphylococcal anastomosis, placing a semi-aspirative drain, and creating a diverting ileostomy.
The surgeon completes the procedure with an end-to-end staphylococcal anastomosis, followed by the placement of a semi-aspirative drain and the creation of a diverting ileostomy.
